title: Faulty Assumptions: Groundwater Modeling Through Anisotropic Fault Zones

research.question: Can one analytical interface condition represent the simultaneous drawdown and normal-flux discontinuities created by fault-core resistance and fault-strike shear flow during groundwater pumping?
research.system: A constant-rate pumping well in a confined anisotropic aquifer-fault-aquifer system where a thin laminated fault separates pumped and unpumped aquifer regions.
research.inputData: Synthetic drawdown and flux fields from a three-zone COMSOL model, parameter sweeps for the leaky fault factor, conductive fault factor, and aquifer anisotropy ratio, plus drawdown observations from the E7 pumping test in northern Portugal.
research.method: Represent the thin anisotropic fault with imperfect-interface jump conditions, derive a semi-analytical drawdown solution through Laplace and Fourier cosine transforms, invert it numerically, verify the discontinuities with COMSOL, evaluate parameter influence, and fit the field observations with DIRECT optimization.
research.benchmark: The interface response is checked against a resolved three-zone finite-element model, and field fit is compared with the Cooper-Jacob solution using SEE, MSE, and MAE.
research.uncertainty: Morris elementary effects describe parameter sensitivity rather than predictive uncertainty; grid-dependency checks assess numerical convergence, while the field interpretation is limited by simplified assumptions and wells located very close to or within the fault zone.
research.conclusion: The imperfect-interface model represents fault-core resistance and damage-zone shear flow in one condition, producing the reported drawdown and flux discontinuities and a closer fit to the analyzed E7 observations than the Cooper-Jacob comparison.

narrative.throughline: A laminated fault is neither only a barrier nor only a conduit, so its reduced interface must let pumping reveal both a drawdown jump and a flux jump.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that pumping lowers groundwater head and that a fault can impede or redirect flow.
narrative.stakes: A model that forces a fault into one isotropic boundary type can misattribute the pumping response and the inferred fault properties.
narrative.resolution: The thin-fault jump condition connects fault-core resistance with along-fault shear flow, survives transformed-domain solution and numerical checks, and returns to the observed E7 drawdown with an explicit interpretation boundary.

background.premise: Fault zones can alter local permeability and thereby affect fluid flow within aquifers.
background.visibleEvidence: A fault plane carries a local permeability contrast beside altered groundwater-flow arrows.
background.whyNow: The viewer first needs the concrete flow consequence that makes fault representation consequential.
background.boundary: The paper concerns groundwater flow near fault zones rather than every geological control on aquifer response.
background.locator: PDF page 1, Section 1 Introduction, opening paragraph
background.visualObject: Aquifer block divided by a fault plane carrying a local permeability change
background.motionPurpose: Reveal the local permeability change so its effect on nearby groundwater flow is visible.
background.stepDetail: Show the aquifer block; introduce the fault plane; change local permeability; redirect nearby flow arrows.
background.transitionBridge: The fault plane expands to reveal its laminated internal architecture.

background.premise: Fault-zone hydraulic conductivity is higher in the fault-strike direction than in the fault-dip direction.
background.visibleEvidence: Alternating damage-zone and fault-core layers carry long strike-parallel arrows beside short cross-fault arrows.
background.whyNow: The directional contrast explains why one fault can behave as a conduit and a barrier at the same time.
background.boundary: The anisotropic representation follows the paper's laminated fault idealization and infinitesimally thin reduction.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.2 New interface conditions for fault zone, opening paragraph
background.sourceExcerpt: fault-strike direction compared to its fault-dip direction
background.visualObject: Magnified laminated fault with strike-parallel damage-zone pathways and a cross-fault core barrier
background.motionPurpose: Lengthen the strike-parallel arrows while shortening the cross-fault arrows to make anisotropy physical.
background.stepDetail: Magnify the fault; separate permeable damage-zone layers from the fault core; show contrasting directional flow paths.
background.transitionBridge: The laminated strip collapses toward the interface used by analytical models.

background.premise: Classical analytical studies separate leaky and conductive faults instead of representing their combined effect.
background.visibleEvidence: One boundary shows only a head step while a second boundary shows only unequal normal-flux arrows.
background.whyNow: The two separate idealizations expose the missing combined interface behavior before the new condition is introduced.
background.boundary: The limitation applies to the classical analytical formulations discussed by the paper and not to every numerical fault model.
background.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 New interface conditions for fault zone, paragraph following Equations 4a-4b
background.sourceExcerpt: In classical analytical studies on leaky and conductive faults
background.visualObject: Paired leaky-only and conductive-only interface diagrams beside an empty combined case
background.motionPurpose: Separate the two conventional continuity choices so their missing joint case becomes visible.
background.stepDetail: Draw the leaky interface; retain its head jump; draw the conductive interface; retain its flux jump; open a third combined slot.
background.transitionBridge: The two incomplete boundaries align on one infinitesimally thin fault interface.

methods.input: A laminated fault zone whose thickness is negligible relative to the aquifer system
methods.output: Coupled jump relations for drawdown difference and normal flux difference
methods.validity: The source assumes the fault zone is infinitesimally thin compared with the aquifer system and relates the reduction to the general imperfect-interface problem.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.2 New interface conditions for fault zone, Equations 4a-4b
methods.sourceExcerpt: Assuming the fault zone is infinitesimally thin compared to the aquifer system
methods.visualObject: Laminated fault strip collapsing into a line carrying separate drawdown-jump and flux-jump markers
methods.motionPurpose: Collapse finite fault thickness while preserving the two directional hydraulic effects on the interface.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the fault-core resistance marker; retain the strike-flow marker; shrink the layer thickness; attach both jump markers to the limiting interface.
methods.transitionBridge: The interface relations become boundary terms on the dimensionless aquifer equation.

methods.input: Dimensional drawdown space time and aquifer-fault hydraulic properties
methods.output: Dimensionless flow equation with leaky conductive and aquifer-anisotropy factors
methods.validity: The source defines scaled drawdown, coordinates, time, and hydraulic ratios to simplify analysis and compare aquifer systems.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Drawdown solutions, paragraph preceding Equation 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: The following dimensionless parameters are defined to simplify
methods.visualObject: Dimensional drawdown coordinates time and hydraulic properties resolving into scaled variables and three control factors
methods.motionPurpose: Map every dimensional state to its scaled counterpart before any transform is applied.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the interface system; pair drawdown, coordinates, and time with their scales; form the leaky, conductive, and anisotropy factors; reveal Equation 5.
methods.transitionBridge: The dimensionless transient equation moves onto the Laplace axis.

methods.input: Dimensionless transient flow equation in Eq. 5a with the imperfect interface relations
methods.validity: The Laplace transform technique removes the time derivative from Eq. 5a under the stated zero initial drawdown.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Drawdown solutions, Equation 6
methods.visualObject: Transient drawdown field folding from a time axis into stacked Laplace-domain decay modes
methods.motionPurpose: Replace time evolution with a transform parameter while keeping the fault interface attached to the field.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the dimensionless field; mark zero initial drawdown; fold the time axis; reveal the Laplace-domain state.
methods.transitionBridge: The remaining fault-parallel spatial variation moves onto a cosine-mode axis.

methods.input: Laplace-domain equation retaining the fault-parallel spatial variable
methods.validity: The Fourier cosine transformation eliminates the retained spatial variable while preserving the symmetric cosine representation used in the source derivation.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Drawdown solutions, Equation 8
methods.visualObject: Fault-parallel drawdown profile decomposing into cosine modes beside the Laplace axis
methods.motionPurpose: Decompose the spatial profile into independent modes that can be solved around the interface.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the Laplace state; project the fault-parallel profile onto cosine modes; isolate each transformed mode.
methods.transitionBridge: The transformed modes enter the three-region boundary problem.

methods.input: Laplace-Fourier equations with far-field and imperfect interface conditions
methods.output: Piecewise dimensionless drawdown solutions for the aquifer in Eq. 12
methods.validity: The transformed equations use vanishing far-field drawdown and the interface conditions in Eqs. 10-11 to define the aquifer solution regions.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Drawdown solutions, Equations 9-12; PDF page 9, Appendix A
methods.sourceExcerpt: dimensionless drawdown solutions for the aquifer can be de-
methods.visualObject: Three transformed aquifer regions joined by the fault conditions into one piecewise response
methods.motionPurpose: Join region-wise modes at the interface so the transformed solution becomes one continuous analytical object.
methods.stepDetail: Place the pumped region; place the fault interval; place the unpumped region; impose far-field decay; connect the pieces through both jump relations.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved mode stack opens onto the real spatial coordinate.

methods.input: Fourier-domain drawdown modes from the transformed solution
methods.output: Laplace-domain drawdown values in real space
methods.validity: The Fourier cosine inversion formula returns drawdown values to the real spatial domain before time inversion.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.4 Solution evaluation, Equations 13-14
methods.sourceExcerpt: Fourier cosine inversion formula is applied
methods.visualObject: Cosine-mode bars recombining into one spatial drawdown profile across the fault
methods.motionPurpose: Recombine separated cosine modes into the spatial response that crosses the fault plane.
methods.stepDetail: Align the mode bars; weight their spatial cosines; accumulate the inversion; reveal one real-space profile.
methods.transitionBridge: The spatial profile remains in the Laplace domain and moves to the time inversion.

methods.input: Real-space drawdown response remaining in the Laplace domain
methods.output: Time-domain drawdown values of the proposed solution
methods.validity: The Stehfest (1970) algorithm provides the numerical Laplace inversion used for the proposed diffusion-type response.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.4 Solution evaluation, paragraph following Equation 14
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain samples resolving into a time-indexed sequence of drawdown profiles
methods.motionPurpose: Return the transformed response to observable pumping time without introducing an unsupported closed-form curve.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the spatial profile; expose Stehfest sampling points; combine the weighted samples; reveal discrete time-domain states.
methods.transitionBridge: The time-domain states become the analytical counterpart of a resolved numerical fault model.

methods.input: A resolved three-zone aquifer with a thin anisotropic fault and pumping well
methods.output: Finite-element drawdown and flux fields around the fault interface
methods.validity: Numerical modeling uses COMSOL Multiphysics with triangular elements refined near fractures and pumping wells.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1 Observations and evidence from numerical simulation, Figure 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: Numerical modeling was conducted using COMSOL Multiphysics
methods.visualObject: Triangular finite-element mesh carrying drawdown shading and normal-flux arrows across a thin fault
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve the thin fault explicitly so the two discontinuities can be inspected independently of the interface reduction.
methods.stepDetail: Draw the three zones; refine the mesh near the fault and well; apply pumping; reveal drawdown shading; reveal normal-flux arrows.
methods.transitionBridge: The resolved fields are repeated on a finer mesh for a dependency check.

methods.input: Drawdown and flux predictions from the selected finite-element mesh
methods.output: Grid-dependency evidence for drawdown and flux
methods.validity: The grid dependency check repeats the simulation with an extremely fine element size setting and reports bounded relative differences for both quantities.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1 Observations and evidence from numerical simulation, grid-dependency paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: To evaluate grid dependency, we also conducted simulations using the extremely fine element size setting
methods.visualObject: Coarse and extremely fine triangular meshes feeding paired drawdown and flux difference bands
methods.motionPurpose: Place both meshes against the same physical field so numerical resolution is separated from interface behavior.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the selected mesh response; introduce the extremely fine mesh response; align the two quantities; show bounded difference bands.
methods.transitionBridge: The numerically checked response becomes the output sampled across parameter space.

methods.input: Model drawdown evaluated across sampled fault and aquifer parameter values
methods.output: Relative parameter importance across space and time
methods.validity: The Morris method (Morris, 1991) perturbs parameter values one at a time and summarizes elementary effects for the anisotropy ratio and two fault factors.
methods.locator: PDF pages 3-4, Section 2.5 Sensitivity analysis, Equations 15-17; PDF page 6, Section 3.3, Figure 7
methods.visualObject: Three parameter sliders generating spatial and temporal elementary-effect bands
methods.motionPurpose: Perturb one control at a time so its influence remains attached to the changing drawdown field.
methods.stepDetail: Hold two controls fixed; perturb one sampled control; measure the drawdown change; repeat across samples; collect the effect bands.
methods.transitionBridge: The parameter effects give way to a time-local diagnostic of the fault response.

methods.input: Time-domain drawdown response at a fixed observation location
methods.output: Drawdown log-derivative signal for fault-response timing
methods.validity: The paper states that the drawdown log-derivative is more sensitive to small drawdown variations than conventional semi-log and log-log plots.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 3.2 Influence of fault zone hydrological properties, paragraph preceding Figure 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: the drawdown log-derivative signal, defined as
methods.visualObject: Time-indexed drawdown trace converting into a separate log-derivative response band
methods.motionPurpose: Expose subtle timing changes without fabricating a curve beyond the source-described diagnostic.
methods.stepDetail: Retain sampled drawdown states; form local log-time slopes; place the derivative signal beneath the original response.
methods.transitionBridge: The diagnostic response becomes the prediction compared with E7 observations.

methods.input: Observed E7 drawdown data and proposed-model predictions
methods.output: Field-conditioned aquifer and fault parameter set
methods.validity: DIRECT is a derivative-free global optimization method that minimizes squared differences between observed drawdown and model predictions.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.6 Parameter estimation, Equation 18; PDF pages 7-8, Section 3.4 Field implementation, Table 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: In this study, the dividing rectangles (DIRECT) global optimization method is employed to estimate the model parameters by minimizing the differences between observed data and model predictions (Jones et al., 1993).
methods.visualObject: E7 observation points pulling a bounded parameter vector toward the proposed-model response
methods.motionPurpose: Tie each parameter update to the observed drawdown mismatch while keeping the field data visible.
methods.stepDetail: Place E7 observations; initialize the bounded parameter vector; evaluate the model mismatch; retain the minimizing parameter state.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted proposed-model response leaves the same E7 observations available for an independent Cooper-Jacob fit.

methods.input: Observed E7 drawdown data and the Cooper-Jacob response model
methods.output: Independently fitted isotropic transmissivity storage and baseline drawdown response
methods.validity: The source applies the Cooper-Jacob solution to the same E7 observations and constrains its directional transmissivities to one isotropic value.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 3.4 Field implementation, Table 1 and paragraph following the table
methods.sourceExcerpt: For the case of the Cooper-Jacob solution
methods.visualObject: E7 observation points pulling a separate isotropic Cooper-Jacob curve toward its fitted transmissivity and storage state
methods.motionPurpose: Fit the classical baseline independently so its later errors are not inherited from the proposed-model calibration.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the E7 observations; initialize isotropic transmissivity and storage; reduce the baseline residuals; preserve the fitted Cooper-Jacob response.
methods.transitionBridge: Both independently fitted responses now meet the same residual definitions.

methods.input: Observed E7 drawdown and residuals from the two independently fitted responses
methods.output: Separate SEE MSE and MAE values for the proposed and Cooper-Jacob fits
methods.validity: The source defines SEE from squared residuals with its degrees-of-freedom denominator and defines MSE and MAE from the same observed-minus-predicted residual set.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.6 Parameter estimation, Equations 19-21; PDF page 8, Table 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: including metrics such as the standard error of estimate (SEE)
methods.visualObject: Two fitted residual stacks resolving separately into SEE MSE and MAE value triplets
methods.motionPurpose: Produce each model's error measures before any ordering is asserted.
methods.stepDetail: Retain both residual stacks; form squared and absolute residual summaries; apply the SEE denominator; reveal one metric triplet per model.
methods.transitionBridge: The two computed metric triplets move onto shared comparison axes.

methods.input: Proposed-solution and Cooper-Jacob solution error metrics for the E7 test
methods.output: Reported field-fit ordering across SEE MSE and MAE
methods.validity: The comparison uses the same E7 observations and reports SEE adjusted for degrees of freedom alongside MSE and MAE.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 3.4 Field implementation, Table 1 and paragraph following the table
methods.sourceExcerpt: The error metrics for both solutions indicate that the proposed solution achieves lower SEE, MSE, and MAE values compared to the Cooper-Jacob solution.
methods.visualObject: Shared SEE MSE and MAE axes with source-reported ordering markers for two field fits
methods.motionPurpose: Compare the fitted models without inventing response shapes or extending the reported field evidence.
methods.stepDetail: Place the three metric axes; add both reported metric markers; preserve their source ordering; retain the lower-error markers.
methods.transitionBridge: The lower-error ordering becomes the evidence handed to the final bounded return.

return.visualObject: The original aquifer-fault-aquifer pumping system with a drawdown jump across the core and unequal normal-flux arrows balanced by strike-parallel fault flow
return.claim: The imperfect-interface model combines fault-core resistance with shear flow in fractured damage zones.
return.claimBoundary: Its field interpretation remains bounded by simplified assumptions and pumping wells located very close to or directly within the fault zone.
return.locator: PDF page 1, Abstract, combined-effects statement; PDF page 9, Section 4 Concluding remarks, final limitation paragraph
return.sourceExcerpt: fault core resistance and shear flow within fractured damage zones.
return.minimalText: Combined fault physics | bounded field inference

visual.primaryObject: Confined aquifer-fault-aquifer cross-section with one pumping well
visual.accentObject: Infinitesimally thin anisotropic fault interface carrying drawdown and flux jumps
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the pumping well and fault plane spatially stable across scenes; magnify laminated fault architecture before collapsing it to the interface; show uncertainty or numerical error only as source-bounded bands, never as invented curves.
